entry.title: S/4HANA 2023 (202302 FPS): Missing entries in table FINS_TRR (EBRR customizing) – how to initialize
entry.content:
Hi all,
we are introducing SAP S/4HANA Service (no Advanced Execution) and want to use a minimal FI/CO integration based on Item-Based Accounting (IBA) and Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR).

While validating the configuration, we noticed that table FINS_TRR (Event-Based Revenue Recognition: Customizing) is missing expected entries / appears incomplete in our client. Because of this, we cannot properly verify or complete some EBRR configuration steps for Service Documents (e.g., deriving/assigning a revenue recognition key for service documents), or we seem to be missing standard reference data.

Is there any documentation guide or how-to?

Best regards,
Lars

entry.title: SF LMS vs Event Tracking Tools
entry.content:
I’m reaching out to gather insights on the tools and processes you use to engage team members in signing up for volunteer events and opportunities. We are currently evaluating systems for volunteer and event signup/tracking — specifically for Inclusion and Employee Resource Group (ERG) events — with an emphasis on ensuring we are effectively reaching our manufacturing workforce.

One of our biggest constraints is that many of our hourly employees do not have business email, and our Legal team continues to advise against sending any communication to personal email, including volunteer event information. We are looking for a vendor that can:

  • Interface with SuccessFactors to capture business emails for salaried team members
  • Allow hourly employees to enter an email of their choosing only if they opt in, without the company sending anything to personal email by default
  • Support QR code and kiosk-based signups for ease of use on the manufacturing floor
  • Provide robust reporting on event participation and tracking

Our Inclusion and IT teams have encouraged us to consider using our SuccessFactors LMS, but I’m advising against this since a learning management system is not designed to function as an event tracking tool. That said, I’d love to hear if any of you have explored this path and what your experience was.

Any insights, vendor recommendations, or lessons learned — especially from those supporting a large manufacturing population — would be extremely helpful as we assess our options.

Thank you all in advance!

entry.title: New Table Builder Panel Becomes the Standard for Story Creation
entry.content:
Introduction

This document highlights an important evolution for the SAP Analytics Cloud Story creation experience and the Table widget.

It aims to guide story designers and stakeholders through the transition to the new Table Build Experience and to provide clear visibility into its planned evolution and roadmap.

With this update, we continue to simplify and harmonize the content creation workflow in Stories, ensuring consistency, usability, and long-term innovation readiness.

 

The New Table Build Experience Is The Default For New Stories

Last year, with the 2025 Q3 QRC release, SAP Analytics Cloud introduced the new Table Build Experience for the Optimized Design Mode.

This redesign marked a major step forward in how tables are created, configured, and consumed in SAC.

The new experience offers:

  • A more intuitive and guided workflow for story designers
  • A builder panel aligned with the Chart Builder paradigm
  • A scalable technical foundation enabling innovation
  • Improved usability and consistency across widgets

All new capabilities and innovations introduced with this experience are detailed in the SAP Community article:

The New Table Building Experience in SAP Analytics Cloud

So, what’s new in the 2026 Q1 QRC release?

With the 2026 Q1 QRC release, the New Table Build Experience becomes the default table experience for any new Story created in this release.

This means:

  • Any newly created optimized Story will automatically use the new Table Build Experience
  • No additional activation or opt-in is required

This milestone reflects the maturity and stability of the new framework.

 

The New Table Build Experience Feature Parity

Between the 2025 Q3 QRC and the 2026 Q1 QRC releases, all previously identified gaps and functional restrictions have been addressed.

Our objective was clear: harmonize and extend table capabilities while ensuring full feature parity with the legacy table experience.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC release, we have achieved full functional parity for optimized stories.

This ensures that:

  • Existing analytical scenarios are fully supported
  • Planning-enabled tables maintain their capabilities
  • Advanced formatting and structural options are available
  • No regression is introduced for enterprise use cases

This milestone confirms the readiness of the new Table Build Experience as the standard going forward.

 

What Happens To Your Existing Optimized Stories

2026 Q1 QRC

If you have existing optimized Stories containing tables, there is no automatic change in this release.

Moving to the New Table Build Experience remains an “opt-in” manual action at Design Time.

You still have time to:

  • Evaluate the new experience
  • Test it in your development or test tenants
  • Validate your specific business scenarios
  • Prepare your organization for the transition

2026 Q2 QRC : be prepared !

Starting with the planned 2026 Q2 QRC release:

  • The New Table Build Experience will be enforced by default for all Stories.
  • The switch will occur automatically when you edit a Story.
  • At view time, the new Table is already enforced for almost all tables in optimised Stories (with a few remaining exceptions).

Further communication and detailed information will be shared ahead of this important change.

We strongly recommend that customers proactively validate their critical Stories ahead of this release to ensure a smooth transition.

 

Share Your Feedback

We want this transition to the New Table Build Experience to be as smooth and valuable as possible.

Your feedback directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please share your thoughts and experience through:

  • SAP Community discussions
  • Customer Influence channels

Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Unlocking Customer 360: Integrating SAP Sales and Service Cloud 2 with SAP Customer Data Platform
entry.content:
In today’s experience‑driven economy, businesses win by deeply understanding their customers, across every channel, touchpoint, and interaction. SAP Customer Data Platform (CDP) plays a central role in this strategy, enabling organizations to unify, enrich, and activate customer data at scale

With the growing adoption of SAP Sales Cloud Version 2  and  SAP Service Cloud Version 2, SAP has delivered powerful, bidirectional integration capabilities that allow companies to seamlessly connect operational CRM interactions with real‑time customer profiles in SAP CDP. These integrations create a rich 360‑degree view of customers that fuels personalization, analytics, automation, and more.

This article breaks down what the integration offers, how it works, and why it matters.

🤝 Why Integrate SAP Sales Cloud & SAP Service Cloud V2 with SAP CDP? 

The integration provides a unified, real‑time data environment where CRM interactions, activities, and master data feed directly into SAP CDP, enabling:

  • A consolidated customer profile from both sales and service operations 
  • Enriched segmentation and activation powered by CRM activity data [
  • Bidirectional data flows to keep systems aligned and up to date 
  • A foundation for personalized engagement across channels and applications

This creates an end‑to‑end data loop that enhances visibility, decision‑making, and automation across the enterprise.

📥 What Data Does SAP CDP Ingest from the Sales & Service Clouds?

SAP CDP collects data from both platforms through predefined event types and mapping schemas.

Inbound Data (Events → CDP)

SAP CDP ingests multiple categories of CRM activities, including:

  • Customer Profile updates (individual customers) 
  • Organization and Relationship updates for account‑level intelligence 
  • Service interactions:
    • Chat
    • Email
    • Phone
    • Cases
    • Registered product activities
  • Sales interactions:
    • Leads
    • Quotes
    • Opportunities
    • Appointments

Each event type is mapped to SAP CDP schemas including Profile, Organization, Relationship, and Activities, providing consistent structuring across data sources.

🚀 Outbound Data (Actions ← CDP) 

SAP CDP doesn’t only consume CRM data,  it can also enrich and push data back.

The integration allows SAP CDP to send profile, organization, case, opportunity, appointment, and lead data back to SAP Sales Cloud or SAP Service Cloud Version 2. 

SAP CDP then receives enriched responses, creating a closed feedback loop where both platforms stay synchronized.

⭐ Key Benefits of the Integration 

1. A Unified Customer Profile

SAP CDP aggregates customer events and attributes from both Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, creating a complete, real‑time view of customer journeys. 

2. Stronger Segmentation & Personalization

Interaction data (including calls, emails, cases, and opportunities) sharpens segmentation models, enabling more targeted and relevant engagement. 

3. Improved Sales & Service Collaboration

Shared data across systems helps teams proactively identify meaningful signals such as churn risk or product issues.

4. Bidirectional Data Harmony

Action‑based enrichment ensures data synchronizes across clouds and remains consistent. 

5. Strong Foundation for AI & Automation 🤖

Integrated customer intelligence enhances SAP’s CX AI capabilities (including Joule), enabling automation and predictive insights. 

How Organizations Can Use This Integration

Customer Service

  • Identify high‑value or at‑risk customers before SLA breaches
  • Personalize case handling using unified interaction history
  • Enrich service tickets with profile‑level intelligence

Sales Enablement

  • Prioritize leads using CDP‑enhanced scoring
  • Improve sales plays with real‑time customer context
  • Track cross‑touchpoint interactions in one consolidated platform

Marketing Activation 📣

  • Trigger campaigns based on service or sales events
  • Build micro‑segments using real interaction data
  • Coordinate lifecycle journeys with behavior‑based triggers

Conclusion: Data‑Driven Experience Management Starts Here 

The integration between SAP Sales Cloud & SAP Service Cloud Version 2 and SAP Customer Data Platform is a major accelerator for companies pursuing customer‑centric business models. By connecting real‑time CRM insights with enterprise‑wide profiles, SAP enables organizations to deliver smarter sales engagement, more efficient service operations, and more relevant customer experiences.

More Info

Suggested learning.sap.com resources and what to look for:

  • Implementing SAP Customer Data Platform 
  • Implementing SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 
  • Implementing and Using SAP Service Cloud Version 2 
  • Exploring the Lead to Cash Business Process in SAP Customer Experience 

Official product documentation

  • SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Integration Guide

entry.title: Comprehensive Overview: Analyze ESG Data Application in SAP Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower as of February 17th, 2026.

The Analyze ESG Data application in SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) has evolved into a central workspace for exploring and reporting sustainability data. Recent enhancements – such as configurable drilldown dimensions, public analytics configurations, extended period selection, and flexible export options – make it easier than ever for users to navigate ESG metrics, create consistent views, and share insights across teams. This overview summarizes the key capabilities that support faster, clearer, and more collaborative sustainability reporting and provides a practical, user-focused supplement to the SAP Help Portal documentation.

Getting Started: “My Views” & Filters

  • The application requires users to select a reporting period and structure before displaying data, ensuring relevant and targeted analysis:

 

  • Once you have selected the relevant Period and Reporting Structure you can also save this using the “My Views” functionality and set it as default. These views are only visible to you, and don't affect other users.

Manage Overviews: Customization and Collaboration

  • The “Overview” dashboard organizes metrics into groups, which are defined in the “Manage Overviews” section. Users can switch between public and private overviews, edit groupings, and add or remove metrics to tailor the dashboard to their needs.
  • Public overviews allow teams to share standardized metric groupings, while private overviews support individual customization.
  • Metrics can be reordered within groups for better visibility, and the system provides clear feedback when no data matches the selected filters. 

Analytics: Deep Dive, Drill-Downs, and Shared Views

  • Users can access the “Analytics” tab by selecting a metric for a prefilled view or starting from scratch via the analytics tab, supporting both guided and exploratory analysis. 

Option 1: Selecting a metric from the “Overview” page:

Option 2: Select the metric within “Analytics” tab

  • Independent whether you clicked on the metric from the “Overview” tab or you selected it directly within the “Analytics” tab, the metric is visualized with a grouping by “Period” per default (if nothing else is applied).
  • Public and private analytics views allow admins and data managers to predefine drill-down configurations for each(!) metric, which can be set as defaults for all users. This ensures consistency in reporting and facilitates knowledge sharing across teams. 

  • The “Manage Analytics” section provides a centralized location to view and edit all saved analytics configurations, with public views editable by authorized users. Any public view can be edited by anyone with the required authorizations.

Advanced Filtering, Drill-Downs, and Export

  • Users can filter and drill down by multiple dimensions, such as period, reporting structure, and energy source type, with up to five drill-downs per metric. This flexibility supports detailed analysis and custom reporting. 

  • Within the “View Settings” you can also create additional filters by “Period”, “Reporting Structure” or “Drill-down Dimension”. Please note that filters within “View Settings” overwrite settings in “My Views” if applied. For filtering within “View Setting” there is a current limit of 12 nodes for multi-selection in hierarchies, set for performance and visualization reasons. Depending on customer feedback this limit might be expanded. 

  • If filtering is applied via the “View Settings” and “My Views” is overwritten, this is also highlighted as an info:

  • Export functionality enables users to download chart data as PNG images and tables as Excel files, making it easy to share insights and integrate with other reporting tools. 

  • Drill-downs for each metric can be set up in the “Manage Metrics” application for up to 5 drill-down dimensions. Select the relevant drill-down dimension(s) and by clicking on saving, this directly calculates the drill downs. Once it has been calculated and saved, the drill-downs can then directly be visualized in the “Analytics” tab of the Analyze ESG Data application

Upcoming Features & Further Resources

  • Key priorities for upcoming releases include displaying targets within the “Analytics” tab, enabling multi-selection in the global filter for reporting structures, support for more chart types with saved custom chart views and saving “Analytics” settings for the whole page across multiple charts further enhancing flexibility and usability. 
  • If you are driving a project with SCT, please upvote or submit improvement requests via SAP Influence to ensure that future updates align with real-world needs and use cases. 
  • For further details, consult the SAP Help Portal and the SAP Roadmap for SCT:
    • Using the Analyze ESG Data Application | SAP Help Portal
    • SAP SCT Roadmap
    • Don’t forget to subscribe the What’s New section within SAP Help:
      What's New in SAP Sustainability Control Tower | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP Sales Cloud V2: How to display Opportunity field in Sales Quote list view?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I’m working in SAP Sales Cloud V2 and I’m trying to customize the Sales Quote list view (table).

My Goal: I want to see the associated Opportunity directly in the table as a column, without having to open each individual Sales Quote.

What I’ve found so far:

  • When I open a Sales Quote, I can see the linked Opportunity under "Related Entities".

  • The Opportunity is correctly listed there as a Predecessor.

  • However, when I try to add a column in Adaptation Mode, I cannot find a field named "Opportunity", "Predecessor ID", or "Primary Reference" to add to the list.

My Questions:

  1. Is there a standard field that I might be overlooking in the adaptation of the Sales Quote app?

  2. Since Opportunity is not part of the standard Key Groups (Schlüsselgruppen) in Extensibility Admin for Sales Quotes, how can I "pull" this information into a custom Z-field?

  3. Would a Determination (Ermittlung) be the best way to copy the Opportunity Name from the Related Entities into a custom text field so it becomes available for the list view?

Any advice or workarounds would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards, Michael

entry.title: Re: Hi everyone, please help with the solution as per bel...
entry.content:
• Activate PMMO in your S/4HANA system (requires business function activation and configuration).
  • Define pegging profiles to control how pegging relationships are established between parent and child WBS.
  • Run pegging to establish the supply–demand relationships.
  • Execute distribution so that actual costs posted to the parent WBS (via production order settlement, GR, etc.) are redistributed to the child WBS elements in proportion to their pegged consumption.
  • Review postings in CO to confirm that costs now sit on the child WBS elements, equivalent to the inventory consumption.

entry.title: Re: SAP PS non valuated project Stock.
entry.content:
Non-Valuated Project Stock means that upon GR the value and the quantity are separated.  

You should use Account Assignment P for direct consumption.  At GR the value is posted directly to the WBS as an expense.

The quantity will be posted to Inventory Management and managed via material movements, but those movements have no financial postings.

The biggest downside is if you want to transfer inventory to another WBS you have to move the value manually with a journal entry.

entry.title: Re: WBS status is released and CRTD at the same time
entry.content: Can you predict when this occurs? If it definitely not standard behavior. The initial status is CRTD and when released the CRTD is deactivated and REL is set. If it is not due to an enhancement then submit a ticket to SAP Support.

entry.title: Getting Started with the cTMS Catalog in SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
Introduction

Welcome to the world of SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud Transport Management service – two powerful tools from the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) DevOps portfolio. In this blog post, we'll explore how these innovative services work together to transform your DevOps processes. You'll discover how SAP Automation Pilot's commands can seamlessly integrate with SAP Cloud Transport Management, automating tasks like managing transport nodes, routes, and requests. Join us as we guide you through leveraging these tools to streamline your software delivery, enhance operational efficiency, and ensure smoother deployments across your SAP environments.

What is SAP Automation Pilot?

SAP Automation Pilot is a cloud-based service within SAP BTP that streamlines DevOps processes. This low-code/no-code engine offers over 300 pre-built commands for various DevOps tasks, while also allowing users to create custom commands. Highly scalable and integrating seamlessly with SAP services and third-party tools, SAP Automation Pilot empowers teams to reduce operational overhead and enhance efficiency across their technology stack. More details about the product can be found at:

  • SAP Discovery Center

  • SAP Automation Pilot Documentation

What is SAP Cloud Transport Management?

SAP Cloud Transport Management service (cTMS) simplifies the movement of software deliverables across different environments and runtimes. It manages the transport of both application artifacts and content, supporting various platforms like Cloud Foundry, ABAP, and Neo. The service enhances transparency by providing a detailed audit trail of changes, while also enabling a clear separation of duties between development and operations teams. More details about the product can be found at:

  • SAP Cloud Transport Management Documentation

Introducing the SAP Automation Pilot cTMS Catalog

We're excited to share how these two powerful tools work together to transform your DevOps processes. Through collaborative efforts, we've developed 16 specialized commands for a dedicated cTMS catalog within SAP Automation Pilot. These new commands cover essential functions including transport nodes, transport routes, and transport requests. This integration enables automatic triggering of specific cTMS tasks, paving the way for fully automated, end-to-end workflows. Whether you're managing SAP projects or looking to streamline your transport management processes, this powerful synergy will help you boost efficiency, reduce errors, and take your DevOps practices to the next level. More details about the catalog and its full set of commands can be found at:

  • SAP Help Portal

 

Step-by-Step Guide

Prerequisites:

  • You have an active SAP BTP account. See: Get started with SAP Business Technology Platform.
  • You've set up your global account and at least one subaccount on SAP BTP. See Getting Started in the Cloud Foundry Environment.
  • SAP Automation Pilot. See Initial Setup.
  • You've set up SAP Automation Pilot in your subaccount. See Initial Setup.
  • You're a global account administrator.
  • SAP Cloud Transport Management. See Prerequisites.

After you’ve made sure that you meet all the prerequisites, you can proceed with the steps listed below.

  1. Access SAP Automation Pilot through your subaccount. Once inside, go to the Inputs section and choose Create.

     

  2. Fill the required fields. Choose a catalog from the dropdown menu, and give your input a descriptive name. Choose Create.

     

  3. Get your SAP Cloud Transport Management service key/binding - access your subaccount, navigate to Instances and Subscriptions > Instances > Credentials, choose service binding, and Copy JSON.


     

  4. Go back to the Inputs tab in SAP Automation Pilot and choose Add. In the dialog that appears, fill the required fields. For Type choose Object, turn on the Sensitive toggle, and provide the cTMS service key or binding in the Value section. Choose Add.

     

     

  5. In SAP Automation Pilot go to the Provided Catalogs section. From there, locate the Cloud Transport Management Service (ctms-sapcp) catalog and choose Commands.

     

  6. On the catalog page, you'll see a list of 16 new commands for managing cTMS resources. These commands allow you to manage transport nodes, transport routes, and transport requests. Choose Create.

     

  7. Fill the required fields. Choose a catalog from the dropdown menu, and give your command a descriptive name. Choose Create.

     

  8. Proceed by choosing Add under input key section. In the form that appears, fill the required fields. For Type choose Object, turn on the Sensitive toggle, under Input choose the one created in Step 2. Choose Add.

     

     

  9. To configure your command, navigate to the Executors section and choose Add.

     

  10. Fill the required fields. Choose CreateTransportNode Command from the dropdown menu. Choose Add.

     

  11. Scroll down to Parameters and choose Edit. Fill the required fields as demonstrated:

    • forwardMode: determines when transport requests will be forwarded. Select one of the following predefined values: Pre-Import, Post-Import, On Success, Manual

    • name: give the node a descriptive name

    • serviceKey: use the name previously provided as InputKey in Step 8

       

  12. Create a second transport node by choosing Duplicate.

     

  13. Scroll down to Parameters and choose Edit. Be sure to rename the new transport node to differentiate it from the previous one.

     

  14. Repeat Step 10, choosing CreateTransportRoute Command from the dropdown menu. Scroll down to Parameters and choose Edit. Fill the required fields as demonstrated:

    • name: give the route a descriptive name

    • serviceKey: use the name previously provided as InputKey in Step 8

    • sourceNodeId: ID of the source transport node

    • targetNodeId: ID of the target transport node

       

  15. After you have done that, trigger the command.

     

  16. Upon successful execution, you should see your nodes and route displayed in cTMS.

     

     

Wrap-up

In this guide, we've explored the powerful integration between SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud Transport Management, demonstrating how these tools can revolutionize your DevOps processes. By leveraging the new cTMS catalog in Automation Pilot, you can now automate complex transport management tasks with ease. This integration not only enhances efficiency but also reduces the potential for errors in your software delivery pipeline. As you implement these practices, you'll find yourself well-equipped to handle the challenges of modern DevOps in SAP environments, ensuring smoother deployments and more reliable operations.

entry.title: Re: Programmatically retrieving lineage/source object inf...
entry.content:
Hello @Shubhamnifty ,

The answer to both your questions are: No, but there is another way.
But they're not pretty.


Let's take them one at a time 

Is there a REST API available to retrieve lineage information similar to what is shown in the Datasphere Impact & Lineage diagram?

No.  The REST Api, as you've already discovered, can only return the metadata-definition of the consumption layer. 

However, the Datasphere Command-line API contains a "READ OBJECT" function, which you can use the output the JSON / CSN file from the Analytical model.

In Datasphere, for reasons that are beyond me, the CSN file for each object contains the full dependency tree.  
However, that file is going to be a lot of work to make sense of.

Here is an example:

In my AM, I have a field called "Client", which reads from the underlaying view 4VF_DKD_ProductionReport:

Much later, I can then look up that view, and find it's definition:

And find another source, which I can then find and dig into, and so on. 

So in theory, the CSN file should contain all the information you'll need, but in a pretty awful format.

To you that programmatically, you need to 

  1. Set up a CLI connection 
  2. Read the CSN file from Datasphere
  3. Transform the CSN file into something useful
  4. Present the transformed data in your desired format. 

And hey, Claude Code might get you there, but it'll be a lot of work 🙂

 

If not, are there any supported system metadata tables/views (for example via SQL access to the space) that can be used to obtain this mapping?

No, the metadata is not stored in the Space tables, but in the SYS schema. 

You can get access to that, by following the steps in this blog post, and access the SYS schema and import your required tables into a space. 

Once you have access to the SYS schema, you'll need (at least) the following tables / views:
(You can see all the available SYSTEM views here. )

  • OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES
  • TABLES
  • TABLE_COLUMNS
  • VIEWS
  • VIEW_COLUMNS
  • CS_JOIN_TABLES
  • CS_JOIN_PATHS
  • CS_JOIN_CONSTRAINTS
  • CS_JOIN_CONDITIONS

You'll most likely need whole bunch more, depending on how detailed you need your reporting. 

Once you have those tables, you can to start from OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES and build your data model from there.

So, sorry I can't be more helpful, but there really isn't a good way to achieve it 🙂

entry.title: How to Set Up and Use the Capacity Scheduling Board in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
entry.content:
How can the Capacity Scheduling Board app be properly set up and used in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

What are the prerequisites for orders to be displayed, and which configuration settings must be checked?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to Set Up and Use the Capacity Scheduling Board i...
entry.content:
Hi,

To correctly set up and use the Capacity Scheduling Board in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the following prerequisites and configuration points must be fulfilled:

Orders are displayed in the Capacity Scheduling Board only if all of the following conditions are met:

  1. The “Relevant to Finite Scheduling” checkbox is selected in the work center. This setting is maintained in the Work Center under the Capacities tab.
  2. A control key is assigned in the routing for the material.
  3. The work center is assigned to a routing for the material.
  4. The routing type must be one of the following supported types:
    - 2: Master Recipe
    - N: Routing
    - R: Rate Routing
    - S: Reference Operation Set
  5. Advanced Planning work centers are not supported by this app.

If any of the above conditions are not fulfilled, the orders will not be visible in the Capacity Scheduling Board app.

The Capacity Scheduling Board displays only pacemaker work centers by default. To define a work center as a pacemaker work center:

  • Go to the Production Version and maintain the work center in the field “Production Line.”
  • Set the work center capacity to finite scheduling to generate capacity requirements for the orders.
  • Use the correct SAP standard formulas to calculate capacity requirements of the work center.
  • For process orders, note that the “Other Formula” in the resource is used to calculate capacity. Therefore, ensure that the “Other Formula” is properly maintained in the resource.

You can see more details on the following links from SAP Help Portal:

  • Capacity Scheduling Board
  • Capacity Planning

Thanks and regards.

entry.title: Re: Replication Flow not being selected as Analytic model...
entry.content:
Hello @NicolasRivas1991 ,

The short answer to your question is: Sorry, there is no way. 

Why is this happening?

In Datasphere, you can download the CSN / JSON file for almost any object. 
For the Analytical Models. that file contains the full dependency tree for that object, down to local / remote tables, including all associations. 

When you're creating a package, Datasphere unravels the CSN file to find all the dependencies. 

For a replication flow, the CSN file contains the connection, the source and the target.

So, to answer your question: It happens because the way SAP decided to build the CSN files dosen't include the replication flows. 
Why did they make this decision? God knows...🤷‍♂️

It happens with every asset?

Same as above - the replication flow isn't stored as a dependency. 

How can I achieve this?

With great difficulty, I'm afraid. 
When you add a replication flow to a package, all the target tables are added.

A solution, while low-tech as all hell, is to keep all your replication flows in a folder (Maybe called Dependency flows) and add them to your package. 

Sorry I can't be more helpful 🙂

entry.title: Re: WebI - Calculate the sum of daily average values acro...
entry.content: "Aufgeteilt in %" is a variable simple calculation. However, we found a workaround. We excluded the Soll Stunden from the original query. Created a seperated universe that gets the Soll-Stunden completly seperated and merge them in the WebI document. Then BO aggregates the CATS Hours and joins the Soll-Stunden after aggregation and we got the single value.

entry.title: Re: Inconsistent Production Version Determination Between...
entry.content:
1. Best practice is you explicitly assign the same production version in both MRP 4 view and Costing View 1 of the material master.
  2. If you need more advanced control (e.g., multiple versions for different plants or scenarios), configure Quantity Structure Control to enforce consistent selection.
  3. Avoid leaving production version determination to “automatic/random” logic — that’s what causes variances.

entry.title: Update and add of conditions price MM in Purchase orders in mass
entry.content:
Dear support, 

We would like to update and add conditions price MM of purchase orders items in mass, not manually with this button 
Is there an application or program that allows mass Update of the Conditions tab at the purchase order level?

The customer would like to update the conditions of purchase order items with the value “C”  
If there is no standard application available, could you please provide a workaround (API, BO, job, etc.)?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Update and add of conditions price MM in Purchase ord...
entry.content:
Hi,

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, there is currently no standard application or background job available to mass update the Conditions tab at the Purchase Order item level or to trigger pricing redetermination with value “C” in bulk. The “Update” function in the Conditions tab is designed for manual processing per individual PO item.

As a workaround, SAP provides the released OData service API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV, which can be used to update Purchase Order items programmatically. Using this API, it is possible to trigger pricing redetermination (pricing type “C”) and update or add conditions in mass through an external integration, middleware, or automated job.

Direct table updates, batch input programs, or custom ABAP developments are not permitted in Public Cloud. Therefore, the supported and upgrade-safe approach is to use the released Purchase Order API for mass processing.

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: RSH unable to assign people on board app- resolved!
entry.content:
For anyone else facing the same issue.

If the configuration is on place, check that the catalogs are setup correctly;
Transaction /n/UI2/flpca search for role and the app and check that the Service Activation Status Text is shown as All services are active for the right APP.

Check APP /UI2/FLPCM_CUST for the catalogs; SAP_RSH_BC_MPLAN_OV and SAP_EAM_BC_PLNBKTS_MNG

Check Services and that there are no Service Activation Statuses inactive.

entry.title: Improving the SAP Community together, one comment at a time
entry.content:
As a Developer Advocate, I have the privilege of working with some amazing colleagues in that team, but also in the wider Developer and Community Relations organisation. For the size of the community, it's a small group, and the folks are working hard to keep the wheels on the bus and rolling in the right direction. But they can only do so much. In this post, I want to highlight a challenge for us all, and how we can, again, collectively, tackle it together.

The challenge

I'm talking about content quality, which to a greater or lesser degree has for a long time been a topic of debate both in the questions area and also the blog post area. My relationship with the SAP Community platform goes back to day 1, with SDN.

Actually, it goes back to well before that - creating and running the first online SAP community in 1995, and co-creating the SAP Developer Network has caused me to never stop caring for and worrying about those platforms, the people, and the content.

Blog post quality

The quality of some blog posts on the SAP Community platform could be better. I know it. You know it. We all know it. The number of people like you and me who worry about this sort of thing is far outweighed by the larger number of folks that make up the entire community.

Over time various folks have attempted to help educate folks so that they create better content. Here are some posts from me on this topic:

  • Help us to help you - share your code (2014)
  • Help us to help you - good questions beget good answers (2019)
  • Monday morning thoughts: a good blog post (2018)
  • Monday morning thoughts: on starting blogging (2018)

These attempts have worked, but only to a certain extent. Not only that, but we have AI content generation to contend with too. While used appropriately, and with thought, this approach can produce great results. But it can also produce poor content, especially when the human component in this equation is not entirely engaged with the purpose and process. Moreover, the ease with which poor content can be produced is astounding. And so the problem has got worse over time, and I can only see that progression accelerating.

The SAP Community belongs to us

Like you, seeing blog posts that don't belong makes me sad and frustrated. So, what should we do in this situation?

Report important issues to the moderators

Well, first, there's a decision to be made. Does the blog post break the important parts of the Rules Of Engagement? What are the important parts? Well, a bit of common sense will help. Does it defame, injure or harass? Is the content illegal? Report it to the moderators.

Feel empowered to call other issues out yourself

In other cases, don't report it. Use your voice publicly, by attaching a comment to the post, explaining briefly what you consider inappropriate, or just plain incorrect. Encourage your fellow SAP Community members to upvote your comment if they agree.

Use the power of the collective, the power of the shared heart, to highlight, educate, and yes, if you want to think of it in this way, to shame the author into adjusting or removing the post and changing their ways.

This is how society works. And the SAP Community platform is an online society. So feel empowered to treat it as one. Call the person out. Gently, politely, firmly, however you see fit. You be the judge - we're all adults here. Don't treat it as a kindergarten and go to the teacher whenever you dislike something.

Why? Because the teachers are in short supply. The moderators behind the scenes are working flat out on an ever increasing load of reports. Some that I would definitely class as important, but many, many others that I'd class as trivial and inappropriate.

Celebrate good posts

Calling out bad posts like this is important. Upvoting such call-out comments is also important.

But as well as calling out bad posts, remember to celebrate good ones by adding comments of praise or encouragement. Or just upvote them. That is the least you can do, it only takes a second.

The power of filtering and natural selection

Calling out poor content by leaving a comment helps educate, even gently admonish, if needed. But upvoting and adding multiple positive comments helps even more, not only to highlight good content, but also to feed the filter mechanisms that can surface content that you want to see, controlled in part by you.

Final thoughts

Thanks for reading this far. Here's a TL;DR (yes, I know it should go at the top, but think of it as the call to action instead).

Stop reporting poor quality content, and similar transgressions. Call the authors out yourself. Everyone is a member, and everyone is a moderator. And if we moderate ourselves with respect, honesty and firmness, we just might turn this giant sea-going cargo ship towards a better quality future for the community platform that belongs to all of us.

Originally published on qmacro.org

entry.title: Designing a Multi‑Tenant Solutions on SAP BTP – Architecture and Prototype Walkthrough
entry.content:
Introduction

Last year, I got an opportunity to work on a project where we had to build  multi-tenant APIs on SAP BTP.  The real-life constraints were the interesting part: multiple customers, strict data isolation, centralized lifecycle management, and the need to keep operations as simple as possible. This experience inspired me to learn more about multi-tenancy, build a standalone prototype and understand the design. I created a full stack application that demonstrates multi-tenancy with CAP, MTX sidecar, SaaS provisioning and tenant-specific persistence. In this blog, I will walk you through the core concepts, the architecture and the concrete steps to get you started.

What is multi-tenancy?

Before touching any code, it’s important to understand what multi-tenancy actually means in the SAP BTP context. In simple terms, multi-tenancy means that a single running instance of your application serves multiple customers (tenants), while keeping their data strictly isolated. The below diagram gives a simple representation of how multi-tenant applications are different from single tenant applications

 Figure-1

 We usually talk about two perspectives:

  1. Provider: The global account owner who develops and operates the SaaS solution.​

  2. Subscriber: The customer (or department) that subscribes to the provider’s application and has its own users and identity provider.

In a multitenant architecture:

  1. One deployed app and shared platform resources serve many subscribers, which optimizes compute usage.​

  2. Tenant-specific data is stored in separate schemas or containers so that each tenant only sees its own data.​

  3. The platform (via services like XSUAA, Service Manager, SaaS Registry, and CAP’s MTX) helps route requests and resolve the correct tenant database at runtime.

In the prototype, I have created a full stack application (following standard CAP layout), but the same principles apply to any real-world business scenario.

How to achieve it on SAP BTP?

​In the prototype, multi-tenancy is achieved by combining - CAP, the MTX microservice and several BTP services such as XSUAA, Service Manager and SaaS Registry. Conceptually, there are three key stages: provider deployment, subscriber onboarding and tenant-specific runtime request handling.

1. Provider subaccount – deployment and setup

On the provider subaccount, you deploy your CAP application along with the multi-tenancy infrastructure. The below diagram show cases the provider subaccount perspective

Figure-2

  1. The CAP application (srv + UI) and the App Router are deployed as Cloud Foundry apps from an MTA archive.​

  2. Cloud Foundry deploy also provisions the XSUAA instance (tenant mode - shared) and a Service Manager instance to orchestrate tenant-specific database provisioning.​

  3. An MTX sidecar microservice is deployed as a separate CF app to handle subscription, onboarding and off-boarding of tenants.​

  4. A SaaS registry instance is configured with callback URLs pointing to the MTX sidecar’s endpoints (for example: onSubscription and getDependencies).​

  5. A technical tenant (often referred to as - t0) is prepared to store tenant registry and runtime metadata.

This setup turns the CAP project into a SaaS-ready multi-tenant application on BTP. 

2. Subscriber subaccount – subscription and tenant provisioning

When a customer subscribes to the application from their own subaccount, the platform and MTX collaborate to create tenant-specific resources. The below diagram show cases the subscriber subaccount perspective

Figure-3 

  1. The subscriber initiates a subscription to the provider’s SaaS app from the Service Marketplace.​

  2. SaaS Registry calls the MTX sidecar callbacks: first getDependency (if any dependent services are needed), then onSubscription to start onboarding.​

  3. MTX registers the tenant in t0 and then triggers Service Manager to provision the tenant’s database artifacts (for example, a dedicated HDI container/schema and binding).​

  4. The CAP model is deployed into this new tenant schema using the deployment and model provider services.​

  5. Finally, a tenant-specific URL is generated and stored so that the subscriber can access the app via their own hostname.

Note: An important operational step is mapping the subscriber hostname as an additional route to the provider’s App Router in the provider space, so that tenant-specific URLs resolve correctly. This is outlined in the GitHub repository as well.

3. Runtime – What happens when a user opens the tenant URL

Once subscriptions are in place, the multi-tenant behavior becomes visible at runtime. The below diagram show cases the subscriber subaccount perspective

Figure-4

  1. A user accesses the subscriber-specific URL which is routed to the provider’s App Router.​

  2. The App Router inspects the host/subdomain, derives the tenant identifier and initiates authentication with the shared XSUAA instance.​

  3. XSUAA delegates authentication to the subscriber’s IdP (for example, IAS or a corporate IdP) and returns an authorization code to the App Router.​

  4. The App Router exchanges this code for a JWT access token and forwards requests to the CAP backend with that token.​

  5. CAP resolves the tenant from the JWT and looks up the corresponding tenant-specific DB binding via MTX and Service Manager.

  6. Application executes the request against that tenant’s schema or HDI container and data is presented to the user.

From the user’s perspective, they simply use the subscriber specific URL and see the data, while the provider operates a single shared application landscape.

Git repository

The full prototype which includes the CAP project, MTX sidecar configuration and step-by-step instructions is available in the GitHub repository The repository contains:​

  1. A standard CAP layout project created using Joule For Developers.​

  2. Multi-tenancy configuration in package.json, mta.yaml, xs-security.json and xs-app.json.​

  3. The mtx-sidecar microservice used to manage tenant lifecycle operations.​

  4. Detailed README with architecture diagrams, local testing and BTP deployment steps.

You can clone the repository, enhance the domain model and adapt the setup to your own multi-tenant scenarios on SAP BTP.

Conclusion

Designing and operating a multi-tenant SaaS application on SAP BTP can look complex at first, but CAP and MTX abstract much of the heavy lifting behind a clean programming model and well-integrated platform services.

By combining CAP’s multi-tenancy features with XSUAA, Service Manager, SaaS Registry and Cloud Foundry, you can run a single codebase that securely serves multiple customers with isolated data. The prototype shows the journey from local testing (with mocked tenants) to a full BTP setup with provider and subscriber accounts, subscriptions and tenant-specific URLs. 

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/get-started/
  2. https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/multitenancy/#enable-multitenancy
  3. https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/developing-multitenant-applications-in-cloud-foundry-environment

entry.title: Using a Lookup Table for Profit Center Determination in Service Contracts
entry.content:
With SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 we enabled a couple of new fields to be used for profit center determination in service contracts: 

  • Bill-to Party 
  • Sold-to Party 
  • Ship-to Party 

Using these new fields can lead to a lot of manual input during period-end closing, because a lot of values of these new fields determine the same profit center. To reduce the effort, I recommend using a lookup table, which is easier to maintain than maintaining every single customer you have. So, here is a guide on how to effectively use a table lookup in the Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Service Documents app to determine profit centers for service contracts. 

Before we can get down to the nitty-gritty, let’s first clarify some of the prerequisites and terminology involved. 

Prerequisites 

Before you implement the Profit Center Derivation, you need to check the profit center determination access sequence described in the following link for the Help Portal page: 
Profit Center Determination for  Service Transactions 

Please make sure you have access to the following apps: 

  • Custom Business Objects (F1712) 
  • Manage Substitution/Valdiation Rules – Service Documents (F7818) 

The above-mentioned apps are SAP pre-delivered under the following roles: 

  • SAP_BR_EXTENSIBILITY_SPEC 
  • SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT 

What are Substitution Rules? 

Substitution rules signify which profit center, costing sheet, functional area, settlement profile or costing variant is applied. 

When you create a service transaction, the system uses the conditions that you have defined in the substitution rule to derive the available target field for the item. When you make any changes to the service transaction item, the system automatically redetermines the profit center, costing sheet, functional area, settlement profile, or costing variant using the conditions which were defined for the item. (Source: SAP Help) 

How and When to Use Substitution Rules? 

You create a substitution rule to determine the profit center based on the material (service product) and the plant. The system uses the plant-dependent product master to determine the profit center. If you change the product, the system redetermines the profit center. From the 2502 release on, you can derive profit centers in service contracts with new fields, such as Sold-to-Party, Ship-to-Party, or Bill-to-Party using the app Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Service Documents. 

Precondition: 

You use the precondition section to identify a field with a certain value to be used for substitution.  

Substitution: 

You use the substitution section to determine the field value to be substituted with a new field value. 

What is a lookup table? 

In the app Custom Business Objects, you create a new business object with a table, in which you can find both, the precondition value for the source field and the substitution value for the target field, which will receive the value found in the lookup table associated with the precondition field. 

How to Use the Lookup Table in the Substitution Tool 

Disclaimer: In this blog, for better readability, we will use the Sold-to Party field as an example. You can do the same process for the Ship-to Party or Bill-to Party fields. 

So, let’s get into it. 

To create a lookup table to use in the profit center derivation, you first have to create a new business object with a table with the desired fields in the Custom Business Objects app. 

In the Create Custom Business Objects in SAP S/4HANA Cloud tutorial group (requires partner/customer license) it shows how to create a custom business object and generate a user interface in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

The table has to contain the source field, in this case the Sold-to Party (SoldToParty) and the target field, which is the Profit Center (ProfitCenter). After adding the fields, you must check the “User Interface” checkbox and publish the business object to be able to use it in the Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Service Documents app. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After publishing the business object, you must add the tile, which you created by checking the “User Interface” checkbox, to your business catalog. To find out how to do that, see How to Add Custom Catalog Extensions | SAP Help Portal. 

You can now open the tile from your Fiori Launchpad and add values for the fields you added. 

After that, you can now use this lookup table in the Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Service Documents app. 

In the Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Service Documents app, you create the substitution rule in the following way: 

Use the Precondition to specify the source field to be used for substitution. For this case, we are using the Sold-to Party. Make sure you set the Operator to “Is not empty”. 

After that, you specify the value and the target field to be used in the substitution. To do so, set the Target Field to Profit Center and use the Substitution Type “Table Lookup”. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

To find the correct value to be substituted, you have to specify the data source and the condition, which you can do using the Source field. To do so, open the value help of the Source field and specify a Data Source and the Source Field. As the Data Source, select the lookup table you created earlier in the Custom Business Objects app. As the Source Field, select the Identifier of the Profit Center field that you used in the lookup table. 

You can now set the conditions that specify when the field will be substituted. To do so, in the Conditions field, select the Identifier of the Sold-to Party you used in the lookup table. As the Operator, you have to use “Equal to FieldFunction”. In the Value field, you then have to select the Identifier of the Sold-to Party (SoldToParty) field in the Service Document. 

You can check the “Overwrite” checkbox to make sure the value you determined for the profit center overwrites any previously filled-in value in the target field. 

After you have activated the rule, the system checks whether the Sold-to Party field filled in the Service Document equals the Sold-to Party field in the lookup table. If this is true, the corresponding profit center from the lookup table will be used in the Service Contract.  

This should save you some time during profit center determination, and if you want to look further, here are some resources from SAP Help: 

  • Profit Center Determination for Service Transactions 
  • Manage Substitution/Validation Rules - Service Documents 
  • Creating Custom Business Objects | SAP Help Portal 
  • Creating Extension Items and Transporting Them | SAP Help Portal 
  • Create Custom Business Objects in S/4HANA Cloud | SAP Tutorials

entry.title: Re: Improving the SAP Community together, one comment at a time
entry.content:
Thank you for the reminder that the community belongs to everyone @qmacro 😃

To add a couple of comments here before anyone asks: 

  1.  DSA compliance/external moderators: just to repeat again - we did not remove the external moderator role (as in external to the SAP Community team) because we wanted to. Due to DSA regulations, we need to always ensure we are compliant, which is why it is currently not possible for us to re-introduce the external moderator role as we used to know it. Having said that, we are continuously looking at how we can open things back up, and we are evaluating options. 
  2. The "Recent Activity" feeds on the platform: hopefully in March we will deliver a change to the filters on the "Recent Activity" feeds on this platform. The filters "Most commented", "Most liked" and "Most viewed" will be changed so that we add a time constraint - that way making it easier to find recently published valuable content, ie. content that got likes, comments or views.

entry.title: Support of a Compact Display for Tables (Live BW)
entry.content:
Introduction

As part of our continuous innovation for Live BW connectivity, we are introducing Compact Display for Tables with the 2026 Q1 QRC release.

Compact Display is designed to simplify complex table structures by consolidating multiple dimensions into a single, structured hierarchy within a row or column.

The goal is to enhance readability, optimize space usage, and improve analytical clarity, especially in enterprise scenarios with deep dimensional breakdowns.

For customers familiar with the “Universal Display Hierarchy” in SAP BEx Query Designer, Compact Display delivers a similar conceptual benefit in SAC’s Optimized Experience, while being purpose-built for SAC architecture and modern UX standards.

 

What is the Feature “Compact Display”?

Compact Display allows you to group multiple dimensions on a row or column axis into a single structured display, enabling expandable nodes instead of separate stacked dimensions.

What it does

When activated on an axis:

  • All dimensions bound to that axis are grouped into one single row or column structure
  • The structure follows the top-to-bottom order of entities bound to the axis
  • Users can expand or collapse nodes interactively
  • The header displays the names of the compacted Dimensions/Measures
  • Display Attributes are not compacted and remain as separate rows/columns

What it does not do

  • If the axis contains only measures, Compact Display:
    • Is unchecked
    • Is disabled
    • Displays the tooltip:
      "You can't apply compact display because this axis lacks dimensions or contains only measures."
  • If no dimensions or measures are bound to the axis, the option is disabled.
  • Compact Display does nothing when only measures exist on the axis.

Why it matters

In real-life enterprise reporting scenarios, tables can quickly become:

  • Wide and hard to read
  • Vertically heavy
  • Difficult to navigate

Compact Display helps you to:

  • Improve density without losing structure
  • Enhance hierarchical readability
  • Provide a cleaner user experience

 

How to Use It

Compact Display is available in the Optimized Experience. If not yet enabled, you must opt-in to the “New Table Builder Experience”.

Step-by-Step Activation

  1. Open your story
  2. Select your Table
  3. In the Builder Panel, locate the Row or Column axis
  4. Click the “…” menu
  5. Select Compact Display

Behavior logic

Scenario

Compact Display Option

No real dimension on axis (only measures or empty)

Disabled, unchecked, tooltip shown

At least one dimension added

Enabled; any new dimension or measure added to the axis will be added to the Compact Display

Last real dimension removed

Disabled again, unchecked, tooltip shown

 

See It in Action

 

Share Your Feedback

Compact Display is an enhancement for Live BW scenarios, and your feedback is essential.

It directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please share your thoughts and experience through:

  • SAP Community discussions
  • Customer Influence channels

Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Header Billing Plan net value is 0
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I am creating a sales order in Fiori app with more than 20+ line items. All items are services using item category CTAD (milestone billing plan).

In the Fiori app, the billing plan can only be maintained at item level. Since maintaining it separately for each item is time-consuming, I save the sales order first then go to "Change Sales Orders" GUI application to maintain the billing plan at header level instead.

However, when I try to set up the billing plan at header level in the GUI app, the net value field is zero.

The sales order itself is complete and has a net value, but I am unable to invoice it because the billing plan value remains zero.

Could this issue be caused by creating the sales order in the Fiori app (where header-level billing plan is not available) and then attempting to apply a header-level billing plan in the GUI application?

What's the solution in this case?

Setting up a billing plan 20 times is not a solution.

Why isn't there header level billing plan in Fiori app?

Apprecite your help.

Best regards,

Zsombor

entry.title: Re: Treasury Related Learning Journey & Certifications ar...
entry.content:
Hi,

Just a quick update on my SAP Treasury path. It turns out the certification world has changed quite a bit since my first one in 2013!

Back then, SAP handed out actual physical paper certificates (which I still have!). While my paper doesn't have an expiration date—and technically makes me a "classic"—it belongs to a different era of Certification 

As of January 2026, SAP has ditched the old-school multiple-choice exams. The new standard is focused on S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Currently, the specific Treasury certification is in a bit of a "loading" phase (likely being updated for the new year), so it’s not immediately available for booking.

I’ll be diving into the latest learning journeys in the meantime so that when the new digital badge drops, I’m ready to trade in my 2013 "relic" for the modern version!
